title: Collaborative Task and Path Planning for Heterogeneous Robotic Teams using Multi-Agent PPO

section_anchors.text: In this work, we present a collaborative planning strategy based on Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization (MAPPO)
Unmanned surface exploration may require not only different locomotion techniques to overcome harsh terrain but also a variety of scientific equipment
As this becomes more complicated for more robots and tasks, especially under the communication delays and constraints of planetary missions, manual planning needs to be replaced by an algorithm
Besides finding an efficient path, the algorithm also needs to allocate a subset of tasks and determine a specific execution order for each robot
A well-known formulation of path planning problems that includes target allocation is the MTSP
To include the full range of subproblems (target allocation, scheduling, and replanning), the problem can be framed within the context of a collaborative game-theoretic approach
To learn a strategy that solves the planning problem, we construct a virtual environment containing agents and targets
The training environment is modeled as a discrete 2D grid
First, the positions of the targets and all agents are included in the observation of an agent
To help the agents navigate towards the sparsely distributed targets, an attraction reward (AR) is introduced
we use the MAPPO algorithm due to its proven performance in cooperative multi-agent settings
To overcome this initial difficulty, the training is divided into two parts, the so-called bootstrap and refinement
Our pipeline was implemented using the JaxMARL framework
The main results were obtained by training three agents with two different skills acting on a 32x32 map
The success rate represents the number of solved environments Ksolved versus the total number of simulated environments Ksims
we compare its performance to optimal solutions found via exhaustive search (ES) in Table IV
A notable advantage of a learning-based method is that the inference time remains constant and is independent of the problem's initial conditions
Having a short and constant inference time for the network opens the possibility of performing online replanning on newly discovered targets
The learned policies achieved up to 86% optimal solution quality with respect to the solve time and up to 92% with respect to the total team effort
The main limitation of this approach is the fixed observation size of the current architecture
This work explored a reinforcement learning approach to the multi-agent global path planning and scheduling problem
